one_line_takeaway: Extending the RESTA smoothing defense to VLMs shows that directional (not isotropic) embedding noise gives a strong, non-trivial security-utility tradeoff against JailBreakV-28K attacks (e.g., LLaVA ASR 50.13%->25.93% with ScienceQA accuracy only dropping 64.07%->61.42%), but the defense is evaluated only against static attacks and lacks a theoretical explanation for why directionality matters so much more for VLMs than previously observed for LLMs.
key_finding: Hard directional noise consistently yields a favorable security-utility tradeoff on both models, while isotropic (normal) noise performs poorly, often at or below the trivial (refuse-everything-interpolation) baseline. Concrete numbers from the paper's own tables: for LLaVA-1.5-7B, hard-noise RESTA reduces ASR from the undefended baseline of 50.13% to 25.93% at sigma=0.005 while ScienceQA accuracy only drops from 64.07% to 61.42% (the headline result stated explicitly in the main text); further increasing sigma to 0.020 drops ASR to 2.37% but accuracy falls to 23.46%, illustrating the tradeoff curve's full range. For Gemma-3-4B, hard noise at sigma=0.475 gives ASR=6.08% with ScienceQA accuracy still at 63.38% (down from baseline 71.42%/56.31% ASR), and at sigma=0.6 ASR collapses to 0.11% with accuracy at 34.12%. In contrast, normal-noise curves for both models track close to or worse than the trivial diagonal (e.g., LLaVA normal noise at sigma=0.020 gives ASR=42.75% with accuracy already down to 39.09%, a poor tradeoff point compared to the hard-noise curve at similar accuracy levels). The authors interpret this as evidence that jailbreak-relevant semantic content in VLM/LLM embeddings is encoded primarily in vector direction rather than magnitude, and that this directional effect is markedly stronger for VLMs than the effect Hase et al. (2024) originally reported for text-only LLMs.
core_contribution: The paper extends RESTA (Randomized Embedding Smoothing and Token Aggregation), a prior LLM jailbreak defense from Hase et al. (2024), to vision-language models (VLMs). RESTA is a stochastic, inference-time defense: it samples k noisy perturbations of the unified text+image input embedding sequence, runs greedy decoding on each, and takes a majority vote across the k perturbed copies to select each output token (Algorithm 1 in the paper). The authors apply this unmodified mechanism to two VLM architectures (LLaVA-1.5-7B and Gemma-3-4B) whose vision backbones project image patches into the same embedding space as text tokens, and empirically compare two of the four noise variants proposed in the original RESTA paper: isotropic (normal) Gaussian noise added elementwise to embeddings, versus 'hard' directional noise, where the injected Gaussian scalar noise is aligned with each token embedding's own direction (e + z*e/||e||_2). Only user-content token embeddings are perturbed; system-prompt/template tokens are left unperturbed, following the original RESTA design. The paper's novel empirical finding is that directional noise dramatically outperforms isotropic noise for VLMs on the security-utility tradeoff, with a 'much stronger effect than previously observed by Hase et al. (2024)' in the text-only LLM setting -- this comparison and quantification for VLMs specifically is the paper's own contribution, not previously reported.
threat_model: Multi-modal jailbreaking attacks against VLMs, where an attacker crafts adversarial image and/or text inputs to bypass the model's safety alignment and elicit harmful outputs. The paper does not design new attacks; it uses the existing JailBreakV-28K benchmark (Luo et al., 2024), which comprises 28K multi-modal jailbreak attacks constructed by applying 14 distinct image/text-based jailbreak strategies to 2000 underlying harmful queries. The defense is framed explicitly as a single inference-time layer intended to sit within a larger, multi-layered agentic-AI security architecture (input/output filtering, model hardening, etc.), not a standalone or complete solution. The authors explicitly flag that only a static, non-adaptive attack benchmark was used and that adaptive, resourced red-teaming (citing Nasr et al., 2025 on defenses being 'severely broken') remains untested -- this limitation is stated by the authors themselves, not inferred.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) evaluation uses only a static, non-adaptive attack benchmark (JailBreakV-28K); the defense's robustness against focused, resourced adaptive attacks and human red-teaming is untested and explicitly flagged as important future work, with an explicit citation (Nasr et al., 2025) noting that many published defenses claiming strong performance have later been broken. (2) The paper offers no theoretical grounding for why RESTA works on jailbreaks specifically -- unlike classical randomized smoothing's certified guarantees against small adversarial perturbations, jailbreak attacks are unconstrained/open-ended and target a categorically different (harmful-content) objective, and the authors say only that 'metaphorical connections' might explain RESTA's effectiveness, calling for further theoretical investigation. (3) Only two of the four RESTA noise variants from the original paper were evaluated. (4) Only two VLMs (LLaVA-1.5-7B, Gemma-3-4B) and one utility benchmark (ScienceQA) were tested; authors call for additional models, benchmarks, ablations, comparisons, and hyperparameter exploration. (5) The paper is explicit that RESTA is intended as only one layer of a larger, multi-layered security system, not a complete defense.

datasets_used: JailBreakV-28K (Luo et al., 2024) -- 28,000 multi-modal jailbreak attacks from 14 attack strategies applied to 2,000 harmful queries, used for security/ASR evaluation
ScienceQA (Lu et al., 2022) -- 4,241 multi-modal multiple-choice questions, used for utility/accuracy evaluation
quantitative_results: LLaVA-1.5-7B hard noise sigma=0.005: ASR 25.93%, ScienceQA 61.42%
LLaVA-1.5-7B hard noise sigma=0.020: ASR 2.37%, ScienceQA 23.46%
LLaVA-1.5-7B normal noise sigma=0.020: ASR 42.75%, ScienceQA 39.09%
Gemma-3-4B hard noise sigma=0.475: ASR 6.08%, ScienceQA 63.38%
Gemma-3-4B hard noise sigma=0.6: ASR 0.11%, ScienceQA 34.12%
Gemma-3-4B normal noise sigma=1.0: ASR 54.49%, ScienceQA 64.30% (near-baseline, poor security improvement)
baselines_compared: Undefended VLM (sigma=0, k=1 equivalent / no smoothing)
RESTA with isotropic (normal) Gaussian embedding noise
RESTA with hard directional Gaussian embedding noise (noise aligned to each embedding vector's own direction)
Trivial tradeoff baseline: stochastic interpolation between undefended model and a model that refuses everything (0% ASR, 0% accuracy), used as a sanity-check reference line in Figure 1
